nativeRange.habitat: The Multicolor Angelfish inhabits steep outer reef slopes, typically occupying small patches of rubble between areas of rich coral growth. It has been recorded at depths of about 20–115 m, where ambient light levels are considerably lower than in shallow-water reef zones.

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on microalgae and small crustaceans in the wild. In aquaria it is generally reported to feed readily; a varied diet is commonly advised, including spirulina, marine algae, a quality prepared marine angelfish formula, and enriched frozen mysis or other meaty items. Feeding small amounts 2–3 times daily tends to help discourage nipping at corals and clam mantles.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jokuyken15; 4958x3306. Sharp, whole fish in frame, well-lit; pale white/peach body, yellow face, dark dorsal/cheek markings. Best overall image quality of the pool.
Photo by Roger Rittmaster; 1024x819. In-situ reef shot clearly showing diagnostic blue vertical facial bars and pale body with yellow snout; slightly soft.
Photo by John E. Randall (SAIAB voucher); 600x400. In-situ shot showing blue facial markings and blue-edged fins; low resolution and dim, kept as variety filler.

summary: The Multicolor Angelfish (Centropyge multicolor) is a small dwarf angelfish from the deep outer reefs of the central and western Pacific, known for its pale peach to yellow body, blue facial markings, and dark speckles. It tends to be semi-aggressive and is best kept singly in a mature, well-established reef with subdued lighting.
careNotes: The Multicolor Angelfish is a deepwater Pacific Centropyge recorded from roughly 20–115 m on steep outer reef slopes, which is reflected in its preference for subdued aquarium lighting and ample shaded rockwork. It appears less frequently in the trade than many reef fish and tends to command higher prices when available, though aquaculture has made it more accessible in recent years than it once was. Sources generally describe it as reasonably hardy once acclimated and feeding readily, but it should be the last fish introduced to a peaceful community and given a mature aquarium with abundant live rock for grazing and shelter alongside open swimming space. Reef compatibility is generally listed as 'with caution': it has been known to nip fleshy stony coral polyps, soft corals, and clam mantles, and frequent feeding tends to reduce this. The species is reported to be sensitive to copper-based medications. Handle with care, as angelfishes have sharp cheek (preopercular) spines that can tangle in nets.
breedingNotes: Like other dwarf angelfishes, Centropyge multicolor is generally reported as a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a group transitions to male. Harems typically consist of about 3–7 individuals with one dominant male; if that male is lost, the dominant female commonly takes his place. Spawning occurs at dusk and is pelagic — eggs and larvae are released into open water with no parental care. Captive larval rearing has been reported only by specialist breeders and is considered very difficult at the hobbyist level.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters: 70 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, omnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family/order, max size (9.0 cm TL), depth range (20–115 m), distribution across Micronesia/Fiji/Cook/Society Islands plus Tonga, harem formation (3–7 individuals), and wild thermal preference (24.3–28.9°C — warmer than the LiveAquaria aquarium range; aquarium-typical 22–26°C retained).

additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (9 cm), minimum tank size (300 L — larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal/265 L), reef caution ('reef safe with luck'), aggression toward conspecifics and similar species, and protogynous hermaphroditism.

fish.breedingNotes: The Multicolor Angelfish is generally reported to be a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a group transitions to male. Harems typically consist of about 3–7 individuals with one dominant male. Spawning occurs at dusk and is pelagic, with eggs and larvae released into open water and no parental care provided. Captive larval rearing has been reported only by specialist breeders and is not considered achievable at the hobbyist level.
